# Campaign Brief: "Watch the Initiative Complete Itself"

**Product:** OrgX live initiative room
**Audience:** AI-native founders and lean startup operators
**Campaign window:** 6 weeks (2 weeks build/capture, 4 weeks run), with a 30/60-day sustain phase
**Owner:** Marketing (Mark) | **Partners:** Product (live room instrumentation), Design (replay player UI), Eng (proof-link infrastructure)
**Frameworks applied:** PAS (pain-first hooks — the audience already distrusts agent demos), Jobs-to-Be-Done (the job: "give me agent work I can actually review and ship"), Message-Market Fit testing (3 hook variants before paid spend)

---

## 1. Campaign Thesis

**One sentence:** Stop demoing agents — let prospects watch a real initiative go from kickoff to merged, reviewable artifacts in an OrgX live initiative room, with every claim backed by a clickable proof link.

The audience has been burned. They've watched cherry-picked agent demo videos that end right before the output falls apart, and they've learned the tell: if you can't click into the work, the work doesn't exist. This campaign inverts the standard playbook — **the proof IS the campaign.** Every piece of content in every channel links to a live or replayable initiative room showing real tasks, real diffs, real artifacts, and real timestamps. We never *claim* the initiative completes itself; we *show* a specific initiative completing, with receipts.

**Core hypothesis:** We believe proof-linked content (every asset links to a watchable initiative room) will convert skeptical operators at 2x the rate of claim-based content, because this audience's primary objection is "show me real work, not a demo" — we will test this with a claims-vs-proof A/B on the landing page in week 1.

**The one thing to remember:** "You don't have to take our word for it — the room is open."

---

## 2. Primary Audience Segments

| Segment | Who (specific) | Pain | Triggering event | Current workaround | What converts them |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **A. Burned Builder** (primary) | AI-native founder, 1–10 person startup, has personally wired up agent frameworks and watched them produce plausible junk | Spent weekends on agent stacks that demo well and ship nothing; can't trust unreviewed output | Just killed (or is about to kill) an internal agent project | Custom scripts + manual review of everything | Unedited replay with visible failure/retry moments and a reviewable diff at the end |
| **B. Lean Operator** (primary) | COO/ops lead at a 5–30 person startup running the company on Linear/Notion/Slack, evaluating agents for real workstreams | Drowning in coordination work; skeptical that "AI employees" produce anything they'd sign off on | Headcount freeze or a quarter where ops work visibly blocked shipping | Hiring contractors or absorbing the work personally | Time-stamped before/after: initiative brief in, reviewed artifacts out, with the review step shown |
| **C. Agent-Curious Skeptic** (secondary) | Technical founder/staff engineer who publicly dunks on agent hype but wants it to work | Wants to adopt, refuses to stake credibility on vaporware | A peer they respect shares a proof link | Waiting on the sidelines | Raw, unedited session access + honest published failure rates |

Segments A and B get dedicated content tracks; C is reached through A and B sharing proof links (distribution mechanic, not a direct target). No two segments receive the same awareness message (see channel plan).

---

## 3. Proof Assets to Capture (Core Campaign Assets — Captured Before Any Copy Ships)

Proof assets are the campaign's load-bearing structure, not decoration. **Rule: no content ships in any channel unless it links to at least one asset below.** Capture happens in weeks 1–2 from real initiative runs (internal OrgX initiatives + 2–3 design-partner initiatives with permission).

| # | Asset | Spec | Used by |
|---|---|---|---|
| P1 | **Live initiative room (public, read-only)** | A real, currently-running OrgX initiative with live task states, agent activity feed, and artifact links. Always-on. This is the campaign's hero asset. | Homepage hero, all CTAs |
| P2 | **Full unedited replay: brief → merged** | One initiative, kickoff to completion, no cuts, real timestamps visible. Includes at least one agent failure + retry left in deliberately (credibility beats polish with this audience). 1 long-form + 90s/30s cutdowns *that link back to the full version*. | Blog anchor post, social, email 2 |
| P3 | **Artifact gallery with diffs** | 5–7 real outputs (PR with merged diff, campaign brief, runbook, spec) each with: the prompt/brief that produced it, the review/approval trail, and a permalink. | Blog, homepage proof strip, email 3 |
| P4 | **The receipts ledger** | Per-initiative metrics from instrumentation: tasks completed, human review touchpoints, wall-clock time, revision count, % of artifacts approved without edits. Published as a page, updated weekly during the campaign. | Blog post 2, social stat posts, sales handoff |
| P5 | **Design-partner witness clips** | 2–3 named founders/operators (Segment A and B) on camera reviewing OrgX output of *their own initiative* live — reacting to the work, not reciting a testimonial script. | Social, email 4, homepage |
| P6 | **"Run it on your initiative" live session** | Weekly open session (recorded) where a prospect brings a real initiative brief and we run it live, unrehearsed. Each recording becomes a new proof asset — the campaign compounds. | Primary conversion CTA in every channel |

**Capture standards:** real timestamps visible; failures left in; every asset individually permalinked so it can be cited, shared, and verified independently; nothing staged, nothing sped up without an on-screen label.

---

## 4. Content Plan by Channel

Each channel has a distinct job, format, and CTA. Same thesis, four different executions — no copy is reused across channels.

### Homepage
- **Job:** Convert skepticism in the first 10 seconds by showing, not telling.
- **Hero:** Embedded live initiative room (P1) — actual task states updating above the fold, not a screenshot or looping animation. Headline: **"This initiative is completing itself right now."** Subhead: "Real tasks. Real artifacts. Click anything."
- **Proof strip:** Three artifact cards from P3, each opening the actual artifact + its review trail in one click. Below it, the receipts ledger summary (P4) with a "see the full ledger" link.
- **CTA:** "Open the room" (zero-friction, no email gate on viewing) → secondary CTA "Run it on your initiative" (P6 booking).
- **Channel-specific rule:** Every number on the page links to its source in the ledger. No unlinked claims anywhere on the page.

### Blog
- **Job:** Deep credibility for Segment A/C; SEO compounding on "agent demo vs production" intent.
- **Post 1 (anchor, week 1):** *"We recorded an initiative completing itself — uncut, failures included."* Embedded P2 full replay with annotated timeline (what the agent did, where it failed, where the human reviewed, what merged). PAS structure: open on the reader's last disappointing agent demo.
- **Post 2 (week 3):** *"The receipts: what 30 days of live initiative runs actually produced."* P4 data — approval rates, revision counts, review time — including the unflattering numbers, with methodology notes.
- **Post 3 (week 5):** *"Demo theater: a field guide"* — names the patterns this audience already mocks (cuts before output, cherry-picked runs, no artifact links) and positions watchable rooms as the standard to demand from any vendor, including us.
- **Format rules:** every claim footnoted to a proof permalink; code/artifact embeds over screenshots; contextual CTA at point of peak skepticism ("don't believe this paragraph? open the room"), not just at the end.

### Social (X + LinkedIn, distinct treatments)
- **Job:** Earn the skeptic's reshare (this is how we reach Segment C).
- **X:** Raw 30–90s screen-capture clips from P2/P6 with timestamps visible — no motion graphics, no music, no caption hype. Hook pattern (PAS): "Every agent demo ends right before the output. Here's one that doesn't." Each clip's reply-1 is the link to the full unedited session. Weekly "receipts" post: one stat from P4 + permalink. Live-tweet one P6 session per week as it happens.
- **LinkedIn:** Operator-voice posts from founders/team (not brand account) for Segment B: "Here's the initiative brief we gave OrgX Monday and the artifacts it produced by Wednesday — review trail included," with P3/P5 attached. Witness clips (P5) as native video.
- **Engagement rule:** community-first — reply to agent-skepticism threads with proof links (when genuinely relevant) at 10x the rate we post; the skeptics' threads are where our buyers are.

### Email (4-touch sequence to waitlist + P6 attendees, plus weekly receipts digest)
- **Job:** Convert warm-but-skeptical viewers into P6 sessions and activated workspaces.
- **E1 (day 0):** Subject: "Watch this initiative finish" (<50 chars). One link: P2 replay. Three sentences. No feature list.
- **E2 (day 3):** Subject: "The part demos cut out." Links to the failure-and-retry segment of P2 specifically. Body names the objection directly: "Yes, it failed at 14:32. Here's what happened next."
- **E3 (day 7):** Subject: "{{first_name}}, this week's receipts." Ledger delta from P4 + one new artifact from P3. CTA: "Bring your own initiative" → P6 booking.
- **E4 (day 12):** Subject: "A founder reviews her own initiative." P5 witness clip + direct booking link. Last touch; non-bookers roll into the weekly receipts digest (P4 delta only, one link, no prose padding).
- **Channel rule:** one proof link per email, plain-text-feel formatting, no image-heavy templates — this audience pattern-matches polished HTML email to vendor hype.

---

## 5. The Anti-Pattern to Avoid in Every Channel: Demo Theater

**Demo theater** = polished, edited, claim-first content that asks for trust instead of offering verification — the exact pattern that created this audience's skepticism. One instance of it anywhere poisons the entire campaign, because the campaign's only message is "we don't do that."

How it shows up, and the guardrail, per channel:

| Channel | Demo theater looks like | Guardrail |
|---|---|---|
| Homepage | Looping product animation or screenshot posing as "live"; unlinked stats ("10x faster") | Hero must be the real live room (P1); every number links to the ledger; if the live room is down, show the dated replay with an honest label — never a mockup |
| Blog | Hero-narrative posts about "the future of agents" with no artifacts | No post publishes without ≥3 proof permalinks; Post 2 must include at least one unflattering metric |
| Social | Sped-up captures with music, cuts before the output renders, engagement-bait hooks ("AI agents will replace your team") | Timestamps always visible; any time compression labeled on-screen; full uncut session linked in reply-1 of every clip; no claim in a post that the linked proof doesn't directly show |
| Email | Feature-list emails, superlative subject lines ("revolutionary"), gating the proof behind a form | One proof link per email, viewable without signup; subject lines describe what the reader will watch, not what we believe |

**Enforcement:** pre-publish checklist item in every content ticket — "Where is the proof link, and does it show exactly what the copy claims?" No link or any over-claim = does not ship. Marketing lead owns the veto.

---

## 6. Success Metrics

Attribution model: first-touch for channel comparison, multi-touch (position-based) for pipeline reporting; known limitation — dark-social proof-link shares will undercount, so proof permalinks carry per-channel UTM-equivalent tokens. Baselines from current site analytics (PostHog); industry benchmarks used where no baseline exists are flagged for replacement with actuals by day 30.

### Primary (campaign passes/fails on these)
| Metric | Definition | Target (by end of week 6) | Owner / action threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Proof engagement rate** | % of campaign-landed visitors who open the live room or a replay (P1/P2) | ≥ 35% | Growth; < 25% at day 14 → rework homepage hero placement |
| **Proof → session conversion** | % of proof-engaged visitors booking a "run it on your initiative" session (P6) | ≥ 8% | Marketing; < 5% at day 21 → test CTA copy variants (3 minimum) |
| **Session → activated workspace** | % of P6 attendees creating a workspace and launching a real initiative within 14 days | ≥ 30% | Product + Marketing handoff; < 20% → joint session-format review |
| **Activated initiatives** | Net-new initiatives launched by campaign-attributed accounts | ≥ 40 | Marketing (pipeline metric reported to founders weekly) |

### Diagnostic (steer, don't judge)
| Metric | Definition | Target |
|---|---|---|
| Replay watch depth | Median % of P2 long-form watched | ≥ 40% (validates uncut format; < 20% → re-chapter the replay, do not re-edit out failures) |
| Skeptic share rate | Proof-link reshares by non-follower accounts on X (Segment C reach proxy) | ≥ 100 unique resharers |
| Email proof CTR | Unique clicks on the proof link / delivered (replaces open rate, which is a vanity metric here) | ≥ 12% per send |
| Receipts ledger return visits | Visitors viewing P4 page 2+ times in 30 days | ≥ 500 (signal that proof is functioning as a destination) |

### Hypothesis tests (decided before scale-up, week 1–2)
1. **Proof vs. claims A/B (landing page):** proof-linked variant beats claims variant on proof→session conversion by ≥ 50% relative. Min 1,000 visitors/arm before calling it.
2. **Hook test (X):** 3 PAS hook variants on the same P2 clip; winner (by full-session click-through, not impressions) gets the paid amplification budget.

**Kill/iterate criteria:** if proof engagement ≥ 35% but session conversion < 4% at day 21, the proof is working and the offer is wrong — fix the P6 offer (length, friction, framing) before touching the content. If proof engagement < 20%, the distribution is wrong — shift budget from publishing to skeptic-thread engagement.

---

### Execution timeline
- **Weeks 1–2:** Capture P1–P5; instrument ledger; landing A/B live; anchor blog post drafted against captured proof.
- **Week 3 (launch):** Homepage hero swap, anchor post, first P6 session, email sequence starts.
- **Weeks 3–6:** Weekly cadence — 1 P6 session, 1 receipts post, 3–4 social clips, blog posts 2–3; weekly metric review vs. action thresholds.
- **Sustain (30/60 day):** P6 recordings auto-feed the proof library; receipts ledger continues weekly; monthly "receipts" blog digest — no launch-and-abandon.

### Handoffs
- **→ Sales:** receipts ledger (P4) + artifact gallery (P3) as deal proof; P6 attendee list with session recordings.
- **→ Product:** session-attendee friction notes weekly; activation funnel data.
- **→ Design:** replay player and live-room embed spec (hero requirement, week 1).

# Launch-Critical Initiative Escalation Playbook

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Playbook ID | OPS-PB-ESC-001 |
| Version | 1.0 |
| Owner | Operations Lead (DRI for playbook maintenance) |
| Applies to | Any launch-critical initiative running in a live initiative room |
| Last reviewed | 2026-06-12 |
| Review cadence | Test via tabletop drill within 30 days of adoption; re-review quarterly or after any escalation that exposed a gap |

**Purpose.** When a launch-critical initiative stalls, the team should never improvise. This playbook converts the three blocker types we actually hit — missing integration auth, free-credit / billing exhaustion, and stuck approvals — into pre-decided routes with named ownership roles, clock-based SLAs, ready-to-paste communication, and a recovery checklist that confirms the initiative is truly unblocked (not just "escalated").

---

## 1. Trigger Conditions

Open an escalation when **any one** of the following observable signals is true. Do not wait for two signals; one is enough.

| # | Trigger | Observable signal | Detection source |
|---|---|---|---|
| T1 | Workstream blocked on integration auth | A task fails or cannot start because credentials/OAuth grant/API key for a third-party integration is missing, expired, or under-scoped; agent or teammate reports `401/403` or "connect your account" state | Initiative room blocker flag; agent error output; CI/integration logs |
| T2 | Billing / credit exhaustion | A workstream's tooling stops because free credits hit zero, a quota is exhausted, or a payment method fails ("upgrade to continue", `402`, rate-limit-by-plan errors) | Vendor dashboard/usage alert; agent error output; billing email |
| T3 | Stalled approval | Any approval decision pending **> 4 business hours** on a launch-critical initiative (we do not allow it to age to 18h again) | Initiative room pending-decision list; approval queue timestamp |
| T4 | General stall | No status change on any launch-critical workstream for **> 8 working hours**, regardless of cause | Initiative room activity feed |
| T5 | Compound stall | Two or more workstreams blocked simultaneously on the same initiative (this is automatically one severity higher — see Section 2) | Initiative room blocker count |

**Rule:** whoever observes the trigger opens the escalation. There is no "someone else probably reported it." Opening an escalation that turns out to be a false alarm is free; sitting on a real one costs launch days.

---

## 2. Severity Levels

Severity is set by **launch impact x time blocked**, not by how loud anyone is.

| Severity | Definition | Examples from this scenario | Initial response SLA |
|---|---|---|---|
| **SEV-1** | Launch date at risk; critical-path workstream hard-blocked, or 2+ workstreams blocked at once (Trigger T5) | Integration auth missing on the deploy-path workstream AND credits exhausted on a second workstream simultaneously | 30 min acknowledgement, work begins immediately, founder paged if unresolved in 2h |
| **SEV-2** | Single critical-path workstream blocked; workaround unknown or expensive | Free-credit exhaustion halting a build pipeline; approval pending past 8 business hours | 2h acknowledgement, same-business-day resolution target |
| **SEV-3** | Non-critical-path workstream blocked, or blocked with a known workaround in place | Integration auth missing for an analytics tool that doesn't gate launch | Acknowledge by next business day; resolve within 3 business days |

**Modifiers:**
- Within **7 days of launch date**: bump every escalation one severity level.
- Blocker **recurs** (same blocker type, same vendor/approver, within 90 days): bump one level and add a "structural fix" action item to the recovery checklist (Section 6, step R9) — repeated escalation means the root cause was never fixed.
- An 18-hour-old approval (the incident that prompted this playbook) is **SEV-2 minimum**, SEV-1 if it gates the critical path.

---

## 3. Escalation Path by Blocker Type

The three blocker types have different owners, different fix mechanics, and different failure modes. **Do not route them through one generic chain.**

### 3A. Integration auth blocker (missing/expired credentials, OAuth grants, API scopes)

Why it's different: the fix usually lives with **one specific person who holds admin on the external system**, not with a manager. Escalating "up" wastes hours; escalate **across** to the credential holder first.

| Level | Who | Action | Move to next level after |
|---|---|---|---|
| L0 | Workstream owner | Check the secrets manager / integration settings first — 30% of these are a re-auth, not a missing grant. Identify exactly which credential, which scopes, which environment. | 30 min |
| L1 | Integration admin (the named owner of that vendor account — maintained in the integration ownership register, Appendix A) | Issue/refresh credential with least-privilege scopes; confirm in writing which scopes were granted | 2 business hours |
| L2 | Engineering Lead | Authority to grant temporary elevated access, approve a service-account creation, or sanction a scoped workaround (e.g., mock/stub for non-prod work so the workstream keeps moving) | 4 business hours |
| L3 | Founder | Authority to contact the vendor directly, accept security-posture exceptions, or re-sequence the launch plan around the integration | Terminal — founder decides same day |

**Parallel action at L1+:** workstream owner immediately documents what work can proceed *without* the credential and continues it. Auth blockers must never idle an entire workstream.

### 3B. Billing / credit blocker (free-credit exhaustion, quota caps, failed payments)

Why it's different: this is a **money decision, not a technical one**. The bottleneck is spend authority, so the path runs through whoever can put a card down — and the decision needs a cost number attached, not just "we're out of credits."

| Level | Who | Action | Move to next level after |
|---|---|---|---|
| L0 | Workstream owner | Quantify before escalating: current usage, cost to unblock for 7 days, cost for 30 days, and whether a cheaper tier/alternative covers the need. Escalations without a dollar figure get bounced back. | 30 min (just to gather numbers) |
| L1 | Operations Lead (holds spend authority up to **$500/month per vendor** without further approval) | Approve paid tier / top-up within authority; or approve switching to an already-paid alternative tool | 2 business hours |
| L2 | Founder (all spend above $500/month per vendor) | Approve spend, negotiate with vendor (startup credits, annual discount), or decide to cut the dependency | 4 business hours |
| L3 | — | Terminal: founder decision is final. If founder is unreachable past the SLA, Operations Lead may approve up to **$2,000 one-time** emergency spend for a launch-critical blocker and must log it for retro review within 24h. | — |

**Standing rule:** any vendor on a free tier that sits on a launch-critical path gets a usage alert at **70% of quota**. Hitting 100% without warning is itself a finding for the retro.

### 3C. Approval blocker (pending decision, e.g., the 18-hour stall)

Why it's different: nothing is broken and nothing costs money — **a human hasn't decided**. The fix is forcing a decision or invoking a deputy, so the path is about decision rights, deadlines, and defaults, not technical access.

| Level | Who | Action | Move to next level after |
|---|---|---|---|
| L0 | Requester | Re-send the approval with a **decision-ready summary**: the question, the recommended option, the risk of each option, and an explicit "decide by" time. Vague approval requests cause most stalls — fix the request before blaming the approver. | 2 business hours |
| L1 | Named approver | Approve, reject, or explicitly delegate — "I need more info" must specify exactly what info and a new decision time | 4 business hours from re-send |
| L2 | Approver's deputy (every launch-critical approval names a deputy at creation time — no deputy, no launch-critical label) | Deputy decides with full authority | 4 business hours |
| L3 | Founder | Decides directly, or invokes the **default-decision rule** | Terminal |

**Default-decision rule (terminal condition):** every launch-critical approval request carries a pre-agreed default ("if no decision by `<time>`, we proceed with option A"). For reversible decisions, the default executes automatically at deadline and the approver is notified. For irreversible decisions (spend > $2,000, external/legal commitments, data deletion), the default never auto-executes — it goes to the founder, who must decide same-day. This single rule prevents the 18-hour scenario from recurring.

---

## 4. Time-Based SLAs and Ownership

### SLA matrix

| Stage | SEV-1 | SEV-2 | SEV-3 | Owner of the clock |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Acknowledge escalation | 30 min | 2 business hours | next business day | Current escalation level holder |
| First substantive action (not just "looking into it") | 1 h | 4 business hours | 1 business day | Current escalation level holder |
| Auto-escalate to next level if unresolved | every 2 h | every 4 business hours | every 1 business day | Escalation Coordinator |
| Status update to initiative room | every 2 h | 2x daily (10:00 / 16:00 local) | daily | Escalation Coordinator |
| Resolution target | same day | 1 business day | 3 business days | Blocker DRI |
| Retro / learning entry | within 48 h of resolution | within 48 h | within 1 week | Operations Lead |

### Ownership roles (assign by name when the playbook is adopted — "TBD" is not a valid owner)

| Role | Responsibility | Default holder |
|---|---|---|
| **Blocker DRI** | One named person per blocker who owns it to resolution. Changes only by explicit, acknowledged handoff. | Workstream owner of the blocked stream |
| **Escalation Coordinator** | Watches every SLA clock, fires auto-escalations, posts status updates. Never the same person as the Blocker DRI (the person doing the work should not also be the person grading the clock). | Operations Lead |
| **Integration admin** | Per-vendor credential owner; listed in the integration ownership register (Appendix A) | Per register |
| **Spend approver** | $0–500/mo: Operations Lead. >$500/mo: Founder. Emergency one-time ≤$2,000: Operations Lead with 24h retro log. | As stated |
| **Approver + deputy** | Named on every launch-critical approval at creation | Per approval record |
| **Founder** | Terminal escalation point for all three paths; default-decision authority for irreversible calls | Founder |

**Out-of-hours rule:** SEV-1 SLAs run on wall-clock time, 24/7. SEV-2/SEV-3 clocks pause outside business hours (09:00–18:00 local, Mon–Fri) and resume next business morning.

**Terminal non-response rule:** if the founder (the last level on any path) does not respond to a SEV-1 within 4 wall-clock hours, the Escalation Coordinator executes the pre-agreed default for reversible decisions, applies the $2,000 emergency spend authority where applicable, and logs both for retro. The initiative does not stay frozen because one phone is off.

---

## 5. Communication Templates

Copy, fill the brackets, send. Channel conventions: escalations and status updates go in the **initiative room thread** for the blocker; SEV-1 additionally pages via the on-call channel; founder-level escalations also go by direct message.

### 5.1 Escalation opener (any blocker type)

> **[SEV-{1|2|3}] [{AUTH | BILLING | APPROVAL}] {Initiative name} — {one-line blocker}**
> **Blocked since:** {timestamp} ({n} hours)
> **What is blocked:** {workstream + the specific task that cannot proceed}
> **Launch impact:** {what slips, and by how much, per day this stays blocked}
> **What we need:** {specific action} from {named person/role} by {time}
> **What we tried:** {1–3 bullets}
> **DRI:** {name} | **Next auto-escalation:** {time} to {next level}

### 5.2 Integration auth request (to integration admin — L1, path 3A)

> {Name} — you're the admin of record for **{vendor}**. The **{workstream}** stream on **{initiative}** is blocked.
> **Need:** {credential type, e.g., OAuth grant / API key} for **{environment}** with scopes **{exact scopes}** — least privilege, nothing broader.
> **Needed by:** {time} ({SEV-n} SLA). If you can't issue by then, reply now so I escalate to {Engineering Lead} instead of losing the window.
> Confirm here with the scopes granted once done; I'll verify and close the blocker.

### 5.3 Billing unblock request (to spend approver — L1/L2, path 3B)

> {Name} — **{vendor}** credits exhausted; **{workstream}** on **{initiative}** halted as of {time}.
> **Options:**
> 1. {Paid tier/top-up}: **${X}/mo** — unblocks in ~{n} min. *(Recommended)*
> 2. {Alternative tool we already pay for}: $0 incremental — ~{n} h migration.
> 3. Do nothing: launch slips ~{n} days per day blocked.
> **Decision needed by {time}.** Within your ${limit} authority — reply "approved option {n}" and I execute immediately.

### 5.4 Approval nudge (to approver — L1, path 3C)

> {Name} — decision **{title}** has been pending **{n} hours** and is gating **{workstream}** on launch-critical **{initiative}**.
> **The question:** {one sentence}
> **Recommendation:** {option + one-line rationale}
> **Risk if A / if B:** {one line each}
> **Decide by {time}**, or per our escalation policy it routes to {deputy name} at {time}, and the pre-agreed default ("{default action}") executes at {time}.
> Replying "approved" or "rejected — because {x}" both count as decisions. "Need more info" needs the specific info named plus a new decision time.

### 5.5 Status update (initiative room, on the Section 4 cadence)

> **[{SEV-n}] {Blocker} — update {n}** ({time})
> Status: {Active / Mitigated / Resolved} | DRI: {name} | At level: {L-n, holder}
> Since last update: {what happened}
> Next action: {what}, by {who}, by {when}
> Launch impact: {unchanged / improved / worsened — say which and why}

### 5.6 Resolution notice

> **RESOLVED: [{blocker}] on {initiative}** ({time}; blocked {n}h total)
> Fix: {one line}. Verified by: {who} — {how, e.g., "task X re-ran green"}.
> Downstream restart: {workstreams resumed and replanned}
> Retro entry due: {date} (owner: {Operations Lead}).

---

## 6. Recovery / Resolution Checklist

Escalating is not resolving. Work this list top to bottom for every escalation; the blocker is closed only when **every applicable box** is checked. R1–R3 are immediate triage and happen in parallel with the Section 3 escalation, not after it.

**Immediate triage (first 30 minutes)**
- [ ] **R1.** Classify the blocker: AUTH / BILLING / APPROVAL / other. Set severity per Section 2 (apply launch-window and recurrence modifiers).
- [ ] **R2.** Assign Blocker DRI and Escalation Coordinator by name; post the Section 5.1 opener in the initiative room.
- [ ] **R3.** Identify and start **continue-work**: list every task in the blocked workstream that does *not* depend on the blocker and keep them moving. A blocker should idle tasks, never people.

**Resolution (per blocker type)**
- [ ] **R4-AUTH.** Credential issued with least-privilege scopes; stored in the secrets manager (never in chat); expiry date recorded with a renewal reminder set at expiry minus 14 days; integration ownership register (Appendix A) updated if the admin changed.
- [ ] **R4-BILLING.** Spend approved and executed; usage alert set at 70% of the new quota; vendor + amount added to the monthly spend review; decision logged (who approved, what amount, what authority).
- [ ] **R4-APPROVAL.** Decision recorded (approved/rejected + rationale + who decided); deputy and default-decision fields verified present for the *next* approval of this type.

**Verification (do not skip — "the vendor says it's fixed" is not verification)**
- [ ] **R5.** Re-run the originally failing task end-to-end and confirm it passes. Name the verifier in the resolution notice (template 5.6).
- [ ] **R6.** Confirm no secondary breakage: downstream tasks that consumed the blocker's output run clean.

**Restart and replan**
- [ ] **R7.** Resequence the workstream: update task dates, re-estimate launch impact, and post the revised critical path to the initiative room.
- [ ] **R8.** Post the resolution notice (5.6); explicitly stand down anyone who was paged.

**Learn (within 48h for SEV-1/2)**
- [ ] **R9.** File a 5-line retro entry: trigger, time blocked, what worked, what didn't, one structural fix. If this blocker type recurred within 90 days, the structural fix becomes a tracked task with an owner and due date — not a note.
- [ ] **R10.** Update this playbook if the escalation exposed a gap (wrong owner, unrealistic SLA, missing template). Bump the version number and re-announce.

---

## Appendix A — Integration Ownership Register (maintain alongside this playbook)

| Vendor / integration | Admin of record | Backup admin | Credential location | Expiry / renewal date |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| {vendor 1} | {name} | {name} | {secrets path} | {date} |
| {vendor 2} | {name} | {name} | {secrets path} | {date} |

*A launch-critical integration with no row in this table is itself a Trigger T1 risk — fill it in during launch planning, not during the incident.*

## Appendix B — Pre-Launch Blocker Prevention (run at launch-planning time)

1. Every launch-critical integration has a named admin, a backup, and a verified credential with > 30 days to expiry.
2. Every free-tier vendor on the critical path has a 70%-quota alert and a pre-approved upgrade decision (amount + approver) on file.
3. Every launch-critical approval template includes: named approver, named deputy, decide-by time, and pre-agreed default action.
4. Tabletop-drill this playbook once per launch cycle: simulate one blocker of each type and time the path to resolution.

artifactMarkdown:
# Experiment Plan: Open-Loop Activation — Day-2 Return for First Live Initiative Room

**Owner:** Product (Pace) | **Status:** Ready for review | **Stage context:** Pre-/early-PMF, B2B, technical-founder ICP
**Decision this plan supports:** Whether to invest in "founder-as-decision-maker" activation mechanics vs. continuing to polish the spectator experience of the first room.

---

## 1. Hypothesis

**Diagnosis of the signal.** Technical founders watch their first live initiative room for 3-7 minutes and do not return on day 2 — *even when the room looks impressive*. That last clause is the tell: the first session is a **spectator experience**. The founder consumes a demo of agents working; they make no decision, contribute no input, and leave with nothing pending on them. An impressive show with no open loop produces "neat" — not a reason to come back tomorrow. The room's value (agents making overnight progress) is also invisible at the moment they leave, because nothing in the session promises that tomorrow will be different from today.

**Primary hypothesis (H1).**
> If, within the first 5 minutes of the first live room, the founder is required to make one consequential, low-effort decision (approve/redirect an agent's proposed next step), and the agents then visibly continue working *because of* that decision after the founder leaves, then day-2 return rate will increase from the current baseline to baseline +10 percentage points, because the founder now has (a) authorship stake in the work and (b) an open loop — work in flight that they directed and that will have new state tomorrow.

**Secondary hypothesis (H2).**
> A day-2 "overnight progress brief" notification (email + in-app) that shows the *specific diff* produced since their decision ("Since you approved X, agents completed Y; one item is blocked on you") will compound H1, because it converts the open loop into a concrete return trigger at the right moment.

**Falsifiable predictions:**
- H1 true → Arm B day-2 return > Arm A by ≥5pp with the decision checkpoint alone.
- H2 true → Arm C > Arm B by ≥5pp; brief click-through ≥25%.
- If decision-checkpoint completion is high but day-2 return doesn't move, the problem is value (the room's output isn't worth returning for), not the loop mechanic — that redirects roadmap away from onboarding mechanics toward output quality.

**Baseline requirement (blocking):** Day-2 return rate for the "first-live-room" cohort must be computed from the last 60 days of PostHog data before launch (estimated week 0, see rollout). A target without this baseline is a wish; do not launch without it.

---

## 2. Target User Behavior to Change

Not "increase engagement." Three specific, observable behaviors:

| # | Behavior today | Behavior we want | Observable event |
|---|----------------|------------------|------------------|
| B1 | Founder passively watches first room for 3-7 min, takes zero actions | Founder makes ≥1 consequential decision (approve / redirect / reject an agent proposal) within first 5 min of the first live room | `decision_checkpoint_completed` with `session_index = 1` |
| B2 | Founder closes tab with no pending state attached to them | Founder ends session 1 with ≥1 item explicitly "in flight, will report back" or "blocked on you" | `session_end` with `open_loop_count ≥ 1` |
| B3 | Founder does not open the product on calendar day 2 | Founder returns within 16-48h and views the progress diff of the room they directed | `room_revisit` with `hours_since_first_session` in [16, 48] and `viewed_progress_diff = true` |

B3 (day-2 return) is the primary outcome. B1 and B2 are the mechanism metrics — if B3 moves without B1/B2 moving, the treatment isn't why.

**Who:** New workspaces whose owner launches their first live initiative room. Technical-founder ICP only (self-reported role at signup ∈ {founder, CTO, eng lead} or GitHub-connected). Excludes invited teammates and workspaces with >1 prior room.

---

## 3. Experiment Design

Three-arm randomized experiment, assigned at **workspace level** at the moment the first live room is launched (assignment any earlier contaminates exposure; any later misses the treatment window).

### Arms

**Arm A — Control (current experience).** First live room as shipped today. Spectator view, no forced checkpoint, existing generic re-engagement email (if any) unchanged.

**Arm B — Decision Checkpoint.** At T+90s into the first live room (after the founder has seen agents working, before the 3-min median drop-off), the orchestrating agent pauses one workstream and surfaces a **decision card**: a real fork in the agents' plan derived from the founder's actual initiative (e.g., "Two ways to scope the first milestone — pick one, or redirect"). Properties:
- One tap to approve, one field (optional) to redirect. Target <30 seconds to complete.
- It is a *real* decision: the agents' subsequent plan visibly branches on it (the room timeline annotates "Replanned based on your call").
- On session exit, an exit panel states the open loop explicitly: "3 tasks running on your decision. Next checkpoint ready ~tomorrow morning."
- Dismissible (founder can skip) — skip is logged; we do not hold the room hostage.

**Arm C — Decision Checkpoint + Overnight Progress Brief.** Arm B, plus at founder-local 8:00am the next morning (window: 16-30h post-session), an email + in-app notification:
- Subject pattern: "Your call on [decision] → [N] tasks completed overnight"
- Body: 3-line diff (completed / in-progress / **blocked on you**), one CTA deep-linking to the room's progress view. The "blocked on you" item is guaranteed: the orchestrator always stages exactly one new decision checkpoint overnight for this arm.
- Suppressed if the founder already returned before send time (they don't need the trigger; log as `brief_suppressed_already_returned`).

### Why this isolates the mechanism
- A vs. B isolates the **in-session authorship/open-loop effect**.
- B vs. C isolates the **return-trigger effect** of the brief, independent of the checkpoint.
- We deliberately did *not* add a "brief only, no checkpoint" arm: a brief with no decision to anchor to is the generic re-engagement email we already believe fails, and a 4th arm pushes sample requirements past our traffic budget. Revisit if C wins but we suspect the brief alone carries it (check: among Arm C founders who *skipped* the checkpoint, does the brief still lift return?— this gives a quasi-arm for free).

### Sample size and duration
- Primary metric: day-2 return rate. Assume baseline ≈ 20% (to be replaced with measured baseline in week 0). MDE = 10pp absolute (20% → 30%), α = 0.05 two-sided, power = 0.80 → **≈ 293 workspaces per arm, ~880 total**.
- If first-live-room launches run ~100-150/week, expect **6-9 weeks** of enrollment. If traffic is materially lower, pre-commit to a 15pp MDE (~140/arm) rather than peeking — record this decision before launch.
- No interim stopping for efficacy before 50% enrollment; one pre-registered interim look for harm only (Arm B/C session-1 abandonment >10pp worse than control → stop that arm).

### Success criteria (pre-registered)
- **Ship Arm C** if C beats A on day-2 return by ≥10pp (p < 0.05) with no degradation in week-1 retention or initiative completion.
- **Ship Arm B only** if B beats A by ≥10pp and C adds <5pp over B (drop the brief's operational cost).
- **Kill** if neither beats A by ≥5pp — and treat that as evidence the problem is output value, not loop mechanics (feeds next experiment cycle).

---

## 4. Instrumentation / Measurement Plan

All events in PostHog (project: OrgX), workspace-level feature flag `exp_first_room_open_loop` with variants `control` / `checkpoint` / `checkpoint_brief`, assigned server-side at first room launch and stamped on every event below.

### New events to instrument (blocking pre-launch work)

| Event | Fired when | Key properties |
|---|---|---|
| `first_room_launched` | First live initiative room starts | `workspace_id`, `variant`, `icp_segment`, `ts` |
| `decision_checkpoint_shown` | Decision card rendered (Arms B/C) | `seconds_into_session`, `decision_type` |
| `decision_checkpoint_completed` | Founder approves/redirects | `action` (approve/redirect/reject), `time_to_decide_s`, `redirect_text_len` |
| `decision_checkpoint_skipped` | Card dismissed | `seconds_shown` |
| `session_end` | First room session ends | `session_duration_s`, `open_loop_count`, `exit_panel_seen` |
| `progress_brief_sent` / `brief_suppressed_already_returned` | Day-2 brief dispatch (Arm C) | `hours_since_session`, `blocked_on_you_count` |
| `progress_brief_clicked` | CTA click | `channel` (email/in-app) |
| `room_revisit` | Any return visit to the room | `hours_since_first_session`, `viewed_progress_diff`, `source` (brief/direct/other) |
| `decision_checkpoint_completed` (session ≥2) | Day-2 decision made | distinguishes "came back and acted" from "came back and peeked" |

### Metric definitions (SQL-testable)
- **Primary — Day-2 return rate:** % of enrolled workspaces with ≥1 `room_revisit` where `hours_since_first_session` ∈ [16, 48]. Hour-based window, not calendar-day, to avoid timezone artifacts (a 3am session "returning" at 9am same day is not the behavior we want to count).
- **Mechanism:** checkpoint completion rate (B/C); median `time_to_decide_s`; % sessions ending with `open_loop_count ≥ 1`; brief CTR (C).
- **Guardrails (paired health metrics — no vanity numbers):**
  - Session-1 abandonment during checkpoint (does the interruption kill the wow moment?) — alert if B/C > A by 5pp.
  - Day-7 and day-14 return (are we buying day-2 with a gimmick that doesn't compound?).
  - Initiative completion rate at day 14 (does pausing a workstream for the checkpoint hurt actual output?).
  - Brief unsubscribe/notification-disable rate (C) — alert at >3%.
- **Diagnostic cuts:** day-2 return conditioned on checkpoint completed vs. skipped (within B/C); return `source` attribution; redirect vs. approve (do founders who *redirect* return more? authorship dose-response).

### Dashboards & cadence
- One PostHog dashboard: enrollment by arm, primary metric with CIs, mechanism funnel (shown → completed → open loop → brief → return), guardrails. Owner: product (Pace). Reviewed Mondays in growth review; decisions recorded against the pre-registered criteria above.
- Week-0 deliverable: baseline query for trailing-60-day day-2 return of first-room cohorts, plus event QA on staging (synthetic workspace walks the full funnel; every event verified in PostHog before any traffic).

---

## 5. Risks and Confounders

| Risk / Confounder | Likelihood | Impact | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Checkpoint breaks the "wow"** — interrupting the live show at 90s kills the one thing working today | Medium | High | Checkpoint is skippable; harm-only interim look on session abandonment; timing (90s) chosen after median watch onset but before drop-off — validate against session-length distribution in week 0 |
| **Fake-feeling decision** — technical founders detect a theater decision instantly and trust craters | Medium | High | Decision must be a real plan branch with visible replan in the timeline; QA rubric: every checkpoint type must produce observably different downstream agent behavior per choice |
| **Novelty effect** — day-2 lift that decays; we buy D2 and lose nothing-burger D14 | Medium | Medium | Day-7/14 retention as guardrails; don't ship on D2 alone |
| **Selection confound** — founders who complete the checkpoint were the engaged ones anyway | High | Medium (analysis risk) | Primary analysis is intent-to-treat by assigned arm, not by checkpoint completion; completion-conditioned cuts are diagnostic only |
| **Brief = spam** — morning email trains founders to ignore or unsubscribe | Low-Med | Medium | Suppress when already returned; guarantee real content ("blocked on you" item always real); unsubscribe guardrail |
| **Overnight work quality** — agents must actually produce credible overnight progress or the brief over-promises | Medium | High | Pre-launch check: instrument current overnight task completion for first rooms; if <70% of rooms produce a meaningful diff by morning, fix that before Arm C (dependency on engineering) |
| **Small-sample temptation** — peeking at 2 weeks and shipping a noise result | High | High | Pre-registered MDE, sample size, and single harm-only interim look, written into this doc before launch |
| **Mixed traffic** — non-founder ICP dilutes effect | Medium | Low | Enrollment filter at assignment; `icp_segment` stamped for sensitivity analysis |
| **Calendar confound** — launch over a holiday week skews D2 behavior | Low | Low | 6-9 week enrollment averages it out; exclude enrollment days where send-day falls on a defined holiday list |

---

## 6. Recommended Rollout Sequence

**Week 0 — Baseline + instrumentation (no user-facing change).**
Ship all events from §4 dark. Run the 60-day baseline query for day-2 return; replace the assumed 20% in §3 and recompute sample size. Validate session-length distribution to confirm the 90s checkpoint timing. Measure overnight-progress reliability (gate for Arm C). Event QA on staging. **Gate: baseline computed, events verified, overnight reliability ≥70% — or Arm C is descoped to a B-vs-A test.**

**Week 1 — Internal + design partner dogfood (n≈10, out of experiment).**
Team workspaces and 5-8 friendly design partners run the checkpoint flow. We watch session recordings (PostHog) for the two qualitative killers: does the decision feel real, and does the interruption annoy. Fix copy/timing. **Gate: ≥7/10 dogfooders complete the checkpoint without prompting and can articulate what their decision changed.**

**Week 2 — 10% canary.**
10% of eligible first-room launches enter the experiment (randomized across all three arms). Purpose: verify assignment integrity, event flow at production volume, brief deliverability, and the harm guardrail (session abandonment). Not powered for efficacy — explicitly no efficacy reads. **Gate: no guardrail breach, <1% event loss, briefs land in inbox (not spam) for ≥95% of test sends.**

**Weeks 3-9 — 100% of eligible traffic, full enrollment.**
All eligible first-room launches randomized until ≥293/arm. One pre-registered harm-only interim look at 50% enrollment. Weekly Monday dashboard review; no mid-flight treatment changes (a copy tweak mid-experiment invalidates the read — batch any fixes for a v2).

**Week 10 — Readout and decision.**
Apply pre-registered ship/kill criteria from §3. Three pre-committed branches:
1. **C wins** → ship checkpoint + brief to 100%, file fast-follow to extend the open-loop pattern to sessions 2-5 (day-7 problem).
2. **B wins, C adds <5pp** → ship checkpoint only; kill the brief infrastructure.
3. **Neither clears +5pp** → mechanics aren't the constraint; pivot next cycle to output-value experiments (what the room *produces* by day 2, not how we frame it) — and we will have the mechanism data (B1/B2 rates) to say so with evidence.

**Post-ship (whichever branch):** 30-day post-launch review against the same dashboard — if shipped variant's effect decays below +5pp by D30 cohorts, reopen as novelty effect.

---

### Assumptions declared
- Baseline day-2 return ≈ 20% (placeholder; week 0 replaces it — this plan is blocked on that number, not invalidated by it).
- First-room launch volume ≈ 100-150/week (affects duration only, not design).
- Agents can stage one real plan-branch decision per first room without scripted theater (engineering to confirm in week 0).
- No human onboarding calls anywhere in any arm, per the goal constraint.

artifactMarkdown:
# OrgX Competitive Battlecard

**Audience:** Founder or first GTM hire selling to technical startup teams (2–50 engineers, AI-forward, already paying for Claude/ChatGPT and probably Cursor)
**Segment default:** SMB / early mid-market. Buyer = user = decision maker. Cycles are days-to-weeks. Lead with time-to-value, not procurement-grade ROI decks.
**Last updated:** 2026-06-12

---

## 1. Category Framing

OrgX is an **agent operating layer for company execution**: it turns initiatives into delegated agent work across product, engineering, marketing, sales, and ops — and every piece of agent work ends in a **reviewed, approvable artifact** (a PRD, an RFC, a battlecard, a launch plan, a PR) with receipts, decision logs, and org memory that persists across sessions.

The one-sentence frame to plant early in every call:

> "Chat tools give you answers. Copilots give you code. Automation tools give you motion. OrgX gives you **finished, reviewed work you can approve or reject** — across your whole company, not just your IDE."

Why framing matters: prospects will pattern-match OrgX to whichever tool they already use. If you let them file you under "another AI assistant," you lose on price to a $20/mo ChatGPT seat. Win the category definition in the first five minutes or fight uphill for the rest of the deal.

### Explicit category comparison

| Dimension | Raw Claude / ChatGPT | Cursor / code copilots | Workflow automation (Zapier, n8n, Lindy-style) | OrgX |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Unit of output** | A chat answer you copy-paste | A diff in one repo | A triggered action (email sent, row updated) | A reviewed artifact tied to an initiative (PRD, RFC, battlecard, PR, launch plan) |
| **Scope** | One person, one session | One engineer, one codebase | Point-to-point task plumbing | Cross-functional: product + eng + GTM + ops on shared initiatives |
| **Memory** | Per-thread; context dies when the tab closes | Repo context only | None — stateless triggers | Persistent org memory: decisions, learnings, artifacts queried by every future agent run |
| **Quality control** | You are the QA | You review the diff | "Did the zap fire?" — no review of content quality | Built-in review/approve/reject gates with validators and acceptance criteria per artifact type |
| **Accountability** | None — no record of what was decided or why | Git history, for code only | Run logs, not decisions | Decision log + receipts: who approved what, when, with what evidence |
| **Multiplayer** | Shared chats at best | Per-seat | Shared workflows, no shared judgment | Agents hand off to each other with explicit contracts (sales → product, eng → ops) |

---

## 2. Where OrgX Wins

**vs. "Just use Claude or ChatGPT directly"**
- **Output structure beats output quality.** A frontier model in a chat window produces a good first draft; OrgX produces an artifact against a contract (e.g., a battlecard *must* have ≥4 objection handlers, ≥3 landmines, a 60-second talk track) and runs a validator before it ships. The model is the same — the harness is the product.
- **Memory compounds.** Ask ChatGPT for a competitive analysis twice, three months apart: you get two unrelated documents. OrgX pulls prior win/loss learnings, past artifacts, and recorded decisions into every new run. Month 6 output is materially better than month 1 — chat tools are flat.
- **Delegation, not conversation.** Founders don't have time to babysit a chat session per task. OrgX lets you assign "produce the launch plan," walk away, and come back to a review queue.

**vs. Cursor / code-centric copilots**
- **The company is more than the repo.** Cursor is genuinely excellent at code — concede this immediately, it builds trust. But a startup's execution bottleneck is rarely just code: it's the PRD nobody wrote, the launch checklist nobody owns, the competitive deck that's six months stale. OrgX covers the 70% of company work that never touches an IDE, and coordinates it with the 30% that does.
- **Cross-functional handoffs.** OrgX agents hand work to each other with explicit contracts — the sales agent's win/loss insights feed the product agent's roadmap input. Cursor has no concept of a marketing deliverable depending on an engineering decision.
- **Review gates for non-code work.** Engineers have PR review for code. OrgX gives the *rest* of the company the same approve/reject discipline.

**vs. workflow automation tools**
- **Judgment vs. plumbing.** Zapier/n8n move data between systems when triggered; they don't produce work that requires reasoning, and they have no notion of "is this output good?" OrgX's pipeline ends in a human review gate with acceptance criteria — automation tools end in "the webhook returned 200."
- **No brittle DAG maintenance.** Automation workflows break silently when an API changes and someone owns the spaghetti forever. OrgX agents operate at the goal level ("produce a QBR deck for Q2"), not the step level.
- **Artifacts, not side effects.** When an automation runs, you get a state change. When an OrgX run completes, you get a document, plan, or PR a human signed off on — something you can show an investor, a customer, or a new hire.

---

## 3. Where OrgX Is Weaker or Not the Best Fit

Be honest here — technical buyers smell spin instantly, and conceding ground on adjacent categories makes your core claim credible.

- **Inline code completion: Cursor wins, full stop.** If the prospect's only pain is "engineers want faster autocomplete and in-editor refactors," OrgX is the wrong tool. Sell OrgX *alongside* Cursor, never instead of it.
- **Quick one-off questions: raw Claude/ChatGPT is faster and cheaper.** "Summarize this email" or "explain this error" doesn't need an initiative, a review gate, or org memory. OrgX's structure is overhead for throwaway tasks.
- **High-volume deterministic plumbing: automation tools win.** "When a Stripe payment lands, post to Slack and update the CRM" should be a Zapier zap. OrgX is for work that requires judgment and review, not 10,000 identical executions a day.
- **Solo founders with no team yet:** the multiplayer/handoff value is muted at n=1. OrgX still helps (memory, review discipline), but the wedge is weaker — qualify honestly and consider nurturing instead of forcing the deal.
- **Process-allergic teams:** OrgX imposes review gates and artifact contracts. A team that hates any structure will experience this as friction before they experience it as quality. If they won't review anything, the core loop breaks.
- **Maturity:** OrgX is an early product in a new category. Raw model chat is more battle-tested for breadth; Cursor is more battle-tested for code. Don't claim parity on polish — claim a different job.

---

## 4. Objection Handling

**"We already pay for Claude/ChatGPT — why pay again?"**
> "Keep paying for it — OrgX runs on those same models. You're not buying a model, you're buying the system around it: artifact contracts, validators, review gates, and a memory that makes month-six output better than month-one. Ask yourself: where does the work your team generates in ChatGPT *live* right now? Who reviewed it? Could a new hire find it?" Then go quiet — most teams can't answer.

**"Cursor already does this for us."**
> "For code, agreed — keep Cursor, we're not competing with your editor. But when your last launch slipped, was the blocker code, or was it the positioning doc, the pricing decision, and the launch checklist nobody owned? OrgX runs the work around the code, and hands engineering-shaped work off with the same review discipline you already have for PRs."

**"We could build this with n8n/Zapier plus an LLM node."**
> "You can wire an LLM into a workflow in an afternoon — and then you own a DAG that produces unreviewed text into a Slack channel, breaks when an API changes, and remembers nothing. The hard part isn't the wiring, it's the quality layer: acceptance criteria per artifact type, review gates, decision logs, and cross-functional handoffs. That's six months of internal tooling you'd be building instead of your product."

**"AI output isn't good enough to trust for real work."**
> "Agreed — that's precisely why OrgX exists. We assume the model's first draft isn't trustworthy, which is why nothing ships without passing a validator and a human approve/reject gate. You're describing the problem with raw chat tools; we're the answer to it. Want to see what an agent's artifact looks like when it *fails* validation?" (Showing the failure path is the most credible demo you have.)

**"This feels like process overhead for a startup our size."**
> "The overhead already exists — it's just invisible and paid in rework. Every doc your team regenerates because nobody saved the last one, every decision relitigated because nobody wrote it down. OrgX makes the overhead explicit and one-click instead of implicit and recurring. The review gate is a 30-second approve, not a meeting."

**"What happens to our data / can we trust agents with company context?"**
> "Everything an agent does produces a receipt — you can audit exactly what was read, written, and decided, which is *more* visibility than you have into what employees paste into personal ChatGPT accounts today. Walk through the decision log live and let them inspect it."

---

## 5. Demo / Proof Moments

Run these in order — each one lands a category point from Section 1. Total: ~15 minutes.

1. **The 10-minute artifact (vs. raw chat).** Live on the call, take a real initiative from *their* company ("competitive battlecard against your top rival," "PRD for the feature you mentioned") and run it through OrgX. Show the finished artifact with its structure, evidence markers, and validator results — then ask them to compare it to what their last ChatGPT session produced and where that output lives now. Using their own context, not a canned demo, is the single highest-converting move.
2. **The rejection loop (vs. "AI slop" objection).** Reject an artifact in the review queue with one line of feedback and show the revised version coming back. This is the moment skeptical engineers convert — it proves the human is in control and quality is enforced, not hoped for.
3. **The memory callback (vs. statelessness).** Run a second related task and point to the exact spot where the agent cites a decision or learning from the *first* task. Then say: "Now imagine this after six months of your company's decisions." Chat tools cannot replicate this on stage — it's your unfakeable moment.
4. **The cross-functional handoff (vs. Cursor).** Show a sales win/loss artifact generating a structured handoff to the product agent, which produces a roadmap input referencing it. Frame: "Cursor does this for code. Nothing does this for the rest of your company."
5. **The decision log / receipts view (vs. automation tools).** Open the audit trail: who approved what, when, with what evidence attached. One sentence: "When your automation tool runs, you get a log line. When OrgX runs, you get this." Also your answer to every trust and security question.
6. **The failure case (credibility play).** Deliberately show an artifact that failed validation and got blocked from publishing. Counterintuitive but powerful with technical buyers: a vendor that shows its guardrails catching bad output is believed about everything else.

---

## 6. Founder Talk Track (~60 seconds, verbatim-usable)

> "You're already using Claude and Cursor — keep both, they're great. But notice what they have in common: they help *one person* go faster, and the output evaporates. The chat answer dies in a tab. The diff merges and the context is gone. Meanwhile the work that actually decides whether your startup ships — the PRD, the launch plan, the pricing call, the competitive positioning — lives in nobody's head and everybody's backlog.
>
> OrgX is the layer above all of that. You hand it an initiative, agents produce the actual artifacts — reviewed against acceptance criteria, not vibes — and you approve or reject them like you'd review a PR. Every decision gets logged, every learning feeds the next run, so the system is smarter about *your* company in month six than it was in month one.
>
> It's not a chatbot, it's not a copilot, and it's not a zap. It's the difference between AI that *talks* and AI that *ships work you signed off on*. Give me one real initiative on your plate right now and I'll show you a finished, reviewable artifact before this call ends."

### Landmines to plant (questions that disadvantage the alternatives)

- "Where does the work your team produces in ChatGPT live today, and who reviewed it before it was used?"
- "When your last launch slipped, was the bottleneck code — or everything around the code?"
